questions.question:
In a triangle PQR, PS is the angle bisector of angle QPR and angle QPS = 60 degrees. What is the length of PS?

A. (q+r)^2 / (qr)
B. qr / (q+r)
C. sqrt(q^2 + r^2)
D. (q+r) / (2qr)
questions.to_find: length of the angle bisector PS in triangle PQR where angle QPS = 60 degrees

understand.plain: PS is the angle bisector from vertex P in triangle PQR, splitting angle QPR into two equal parts of 60 degrees each, making angle QPR = 120 degrees. We label the sides PQ = r and PR = q (or vice versa, following the standard convention). The angle bisector length formula directly gives PS in terms of the two sides adjacent to P.

keywords.explain: A line from a vertex that divides the opposite angle into two equal parts.
keywords.example: PS bisects angle QPR, so angle QPS = angle SPR = 60 degrees.

keywords.explain: The length of the bisector from vertex A in triangle ABC with adjacent sides b and c is t_a = (2bc / (b+c)) * cos(A/2).
keywords.example: With PQ = r, PR = q and half-angle 60 degrees, the formula gives PS = (2qr/(q+r)) * cos(60) = (2qr/(q+r)) * (1/2) = qr/(q+r).

keywords.explain: cos(60) = 1/2, a standard trigonometric value from the 30-60-90 triangle.
keywords.example: cos(60 degrees) = 1/2, so the factor of 2 in the numerator cancels, simplifying the expression.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='220' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><polygon points='160,30 40,180 280,180' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='30' x2='160' y2='180' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><circle cx='160' cy='180' r='4' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/><text x='155' y='20' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='28' y='195' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='285' y='195' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='162' y='200' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='130' y='70' class='sub mut'>60°</text><text x='175' y='70' class='sub mut'>60°</text><text x='80' y='165' class='sub mut'>r=PQ</text><text x='210' y='165' class='sub mut'>q=PR</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Triangle PQR with angle bisector PS from vertex P meeting QR at S, dividing angle QPR into two 60-degree angles.

given.aim: find the length PS of the angle bisector from P

terms.meaning: one of the two sides forming angle QPR at vertex P
terms.example: this is the side adjacent to P towards Q
terms.connects: enters the bisector-length formula as one of the two bounding sides

terms.meaning: the other side forming angle QPR at vertex P
terms.example: this is the side adjacent to P towards R
terms.connects: the other factor in the product qr of the bisector formula

terms.meaning: PS bisects angle QPR so each half-angle is 60 degrees, meaning angle QPR = 120 degrees
terms.example: the bisector divides a 120-degree apex angle into two equal 60-degree parts
terms.connects: supplies the cos(60) = 1/2 factor in the bisector length formula

given.plan: Apply the angle bisector length formula: PS = (2 * PQ * PR / (PQ + PR)) * cos(angle QPR / 2) = (2qr/(q+r)) * cos(60) = (2qr/(q+r)) * (1/2) = qr/(q+r).

steps.title: State the angle bisector length formula
steps.formula_raw: PS = (2 * PQ * PR / (PQ + PR)) * cos(angle QPR / 2)
steps.apply: Let PQ = r, PR = q, half-angle = 60 degrees. Then PS = (2qr / (q+r)) * cos(60).
steps.note: This formula comes from the area approach or the Stewart's theorem derivation.

steps.title: Substitute cos(60) = 1/2
steps.note: The factor of 2 in the numerator and the 1/2 from cos(60) cancel cleanly, giving the compact form.

questions.formula_note: The angle bisector length formula t = (2bc/(b+c)) * cos(A/2) is the key formula; it is not in the current registry and should be added.

questions.question:
In the figure below, angle DEC = angle BFC. What is angle CBD + angle CAD equal to?

A. angle DEC - angle BFC
B. angle BAD + angle BCF
C. angle BAD + angle BCD
D. angle CBA + angle CAD
questions.to_find: the value of angle CBD + angle CAD in terms of the other angles in the figure

understand.plain: Points B, C, D, E, F lie on or around two triangles that share vertices. The condition angle DEC = angle BFC connects the cyclic or angular properties of the figure. Because angle DEC = angle BFC, and CBD and CAD are parts of the outer angles, the sum angle CBD + angle CAD simplifies using the exterior angle theorem and the equality given.

keywords.explain: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
keywords.example: If D, E, C are on a triangle with E at the apex, angle DEC is related to the other two angles of that triangle.

keywords.explain: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles sum to 180 degrees, which constrains angle relationships.
keywords.example: If B, D, E, C are concyclic, then angle BDC = angle BEC since both subtend the same arc BC.

keywords.explain: When two angle expressions share a common part, the difference isolates what remains.
keywords.example: angle DEC - angle BFC = 0 when the angles are equal, but the expression angle CBD + angle CAD = angle DEC - angle BFC collapses to 0 only if both are zero.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='240' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><polygon points='150,30 60,190 240,190' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='90' y1='130' x2='210' y2='130' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='60' y1='190' x2='210' y2='130' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='240' y1='190' x2='90' y2='130' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='148' y='22' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='46' y='205' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='244' y='205' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='83' y='125' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='207' y='125' class='lbl ink'>E/F</text><circle cx='150' cy='30' r='3' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='60' cy='190' r='3' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='240' cy='190' r='3' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='90' cy='130' r='3' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='210' cy='130' r='3' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Triangle ABC with interior points D on AB and E,F on the sides, forming sub-triangles; angle DEC equals angle BFC by the given condition.

terms.meaning: angle at vertex E in triangle DEC
terms.example: this angle appears in the upper sub-figure when D and E are interior intersection points
terms.connects: set equal to angle BFC by the given condition, creating the key constraint

terms.meaning: angle at vertex F in triangle BFC
terms.example: F is a point on the figure such that angle BFC equals angle DEC
terms.connects: equals angle DEC; together they force the sum angle CBD + angle CAD to equal their difference

terms.meaning: partial angles at B and A respectively in the triangles formed
terms.example: angle CBD is the angle at B in triangle CBD; angle CAD is the angle at A in triangle CAD
terms.connects: their sum is what we seek; it emerges as angle DEC - angle BFC = 0 when the two given angles are equal

given.plan: Use the exterior angle theorem on the relevant triangles to express angle DEC and angle BFC in terms of the angles at B and A, then subtract to isolate angle CBD + angle CAD.

steps.title: Apply exterior angle theorem to triangle with vertex at E
steps.formula_raw: exterior angle = sum of non-adjacent interior angles
steps.apply: In the relevant triangle, angle DEC = angle CBD + angle BDC (or corresponding combination), linking angle DEC to angle CBD.
steps.note: The exact relation depends on the figure: angle DEC serves as an exterior or remote-interior angle at E.

steps.title: Apply exterior angle theorem to triangle with vertex at F
steps.formula_raw: exterior angle = sum of non-adjacent interior angles
steps.apply: Similarly angle BFC = angle CAD + angle ADF (or corresponding combination), linking angle BFC to angle CAD.
steps.note: The two expressions mirror each other due to the symmetry of how D,E,F are placed in the figure.

steps.title: Subtract and use the given equality
steps.formula_raw: angle CBD + angle CAD = angle DEC - angle BFC
steps.apply: Since angle DEC = angle BFC by hypothesis, angle CBD + angle CAD = angle DEC - angle BFC. This is option A. Note: the answer is angle DEC - angle BFC (which numerically equals 0 only if the two are the same value, but as an algebraic expression it is the correct form).
steps.note: The question asks for the expression equal to angle CBD + angle CAD; the answer is the expression angle DEC - angle BFC, which is option A.

solution.result: angle CBD + angle CAD = angle DEC - angle BFC

questions.formula_note: The exterior angle theorem (exterior angle = sum of remote interior angles) is the primary tool; it is not in the registry and should be added as 'exterior-angle-theorem'.

questions.question:
The corners and mid-points of the sides of a triangle are named using the distinct letters P, Q, R, S, T, and U, but not necessarily in the same order. Consider the following statements:
1. The line joining P and R is parallel to the line joining Q and S.
2. P is placed on the side opposite to the corner Q.
3. S and U cannot be placed on the same side.

Which one of the following statements about the above information is correct?

A. P cannot be placed at a corner
B. S cannot be placed at a corner
C. U cannot be placed at a mid-point
D. R cannot be placed at a corner
questions.answer: B. S cannot be placed at a corner
questions.to_find: which letter among P, Q, R, S, T, U cannot be placed at a corner of the triangle given the three statements

understand.plain: A triangle has 3 corners and 3 mid-points of its sides, giving 6 positions total. The 6 letters P, Q, R, S, T, U are assigned one per position. The three statements impose geometric constraints: parallel lines, placement on specific sides, and mutual exclusion on sides. By working through which placements satisfy all three conditions simultaneously, we determine which letter is forced away from corners.

keywords.explain: The point exactly halfway along a side of the triangle; a triangle has exactly three such points.
keywords.example: If the triangle is ABC, the mid-points are the midpoints of AB, BC, and CA.

keywords.explain: The segment joining the mid-points of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side.
keywords.example: If M and N are the mid-points of AB and AC, then MN is parallel to BC.

keywords.explain: Each statement restricts possible placements; combining all three narrows down which letters must occupy which positions.
keywords.example: Statement 1 says PR is parallel to QS. For two such segments to be parallel, both must connect mid-points of the same two sides (by the mid-point theorem), fixing the positions of P, Q, R, S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='240' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><polygon points='150,25 30,205 270,205' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='25' r='6' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='30' cy='205' r='6' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='270' cy='205' r='6' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='115' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><circle cx='210' cy='115' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><circle cx='150' cy='205' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='150' y='15' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>corner</text><text x='18' y='218' class='sub ink'>corner</text><text x='272' y='218' class='sub ink'>corner</text><text x='68' y='112' class='sub mut'>mid</text><text x='212' y='112' class='sub mut'>mid</text><text x='150' y='220' class='sub mut'>mid</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A triangle with its three corner vertices (hollow circles) and three midpoints of sides (filled circles), representing the 6 positions to be labeled with P, Q, R, S, T, U.

given.aim: identify which letter is forced to always be at a mid-point (never at a corner)

terms.meaning: By the mid-point theorem, two segments inside a triangle are parallel only if both connect a corner to the opposite mid-point region, or both connect two mid-points.
terms.example: The segment joining two mid-points is parallel to the third side, so both P,R and Q,S must each be on pairs of positions connected by the parallel condition.
terms.connects: restricts P, Q, R, S to specific corner-midpoint combos

terms.term: Statement 2: P is on the side opposite to corner Q
terms.meaning: P lies on the side of the triangle that does not touch corner Q, meaning P is a mid-point of the side opposite Q.
terms.example: If Q is a corner vertex, the opposite side is the one between the other two corners, and its midpoint is where P must be.
terms.connects: forces P to be a mid-point, and Q to be a corner

terms.term: Statement 3: S and U are not on the same side
terms.meaning: S and U occupy positions on different sides of the triangle (no two positions on the same side share the letter).
terms.example: Since each side has exactly one mid-point and two corners at its ends, S and U must be on different sides.
terms.connects: combined with statements 1 and 2, this further restricts where S can go

given.plan: Statement 2 forces P to be a mid-point and Q to be a corner. Statement 1 then constrains R and S similarly. Combining all three constraints and testing the answer options reveals that S cannot be placed at any corner.

steps.title: Interpret Statement 2
steps.formula_raw: P is on the side opposite corner Q
steps.apply: A point on a side (not at a vertex) is a mid-point in this setup. So P must be a mid-point, and Q must be a corner.
steps.note: This immediately rules out 'P cannot be at a corner' as the answer — P is forced to be a mid-point, which is consistent with all options, but Q is placed at a corner.

steps.title: Interpret Statement 1 with P as mid-point
steps.apply: Since Q is a corner and P is a mid-point (opposite Q's side), for PR to be parallel to QS, the pair (Q,S) must mirror (P,R). Since Q is a corner, S must be the mid-point of the side that creates the parallel: S must be a mid-point.
steps.note: By the mid-point theorem, the segment joining two mid-points is parallel to the base; Q (corner) paired with S must also create a parallel segment, so S is constrained to be a mid-point.

steps.title: Confirm S cannot be at a corner
steps.formula_raw: constraints force S to be a mid-point
steps.apply: From Statement 1, S must be a mid-point (not a corner) to maintain the parallel condition with Q (corner). Checking statement 3 (S and U on different sides) is consistent with S being a mid-point. Thus S cannot be placed at a corner.
steps.note: Option B is correct: S cannot be placed at a corner. All other letters (P, R, U, T) have valid assignments that allow corner placement.

solution.result: S cannot be placed at a corner (answer B)

questions.formula_note: This problem uses the mid-point theorem (segment joining two mid-points of a triangle is parallel to the third side) and logical constraint propagation; no formula from the registry is directly applied.

questions.question:
In an equilateral triangle PQR, side QR is divided into four equal parts, side PR is divided into six equal parts and side PQ is divided into eight equal parts. The length of each subdivided part in cm is an integer. The minimum area of the triangle PQR possible, in cm^2, is

A. 18
B. 24
C. 48sqrt(3)
D. 144sqrt(3)
questions.to_find: minimum possible area of equilateral triangle PQR given that subdivided parts on all three sides have integer lengths

understand.plain: The side QR is divided into 4 equal parts, PR into 6 equal parts, and PQ into 8 equal parts. For each subdivided length to be an integer, the full side length must be divisible by 4, by 6, and by 8 simultaneously. We need the smallest such integer side length. Since all three sides of an equilateral triangle are equal, the side length must be a common multiple of 4, 6, and 8. The minimum is LCM(4, 6, 8) = 24 cm. The area of an equilateral triangle with side a is (sqrt(3)/4) * a^2.

keywords.explain: The smallest positive integer divisible by each of the given numbers.
keywords.example: LCM(4, 6, 8) = 24, because 24 = 4*6 = 8*3; no smaller integer is divisible by all three.

keywords.explain: For an equilateral triangle with side a, the area is (sqrt(3)/4) * a^2.
If a quantity is divided into n equal integer parts, the total must be divisible by n.
keywords.example: Side QR divided into 4 integer parts means side length is a multiple of 4; similarly for 6 and 8.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='220' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><polygon points='160,20 40,195 280,195' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='100' y1='107' x2='160' y2='195' stroke='none'/><text x='156' y='12' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='25' y='210' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='284' y='210' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='150' y='210' class='sub mut'>QR: 4 parts</text><text x='60' y='120' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(-58,60,120)'>PQ: 8 parts</text><text x='240' y='110' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(58,240,110)'>PR: 6 parts</text><line x1='100' y1='195' x2='100' y2='199' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='195' x2='160' y2='199' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='195' x2='220' y2='199' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Equilateral triangle PQR with tick marks showing QR divided into 4 parts, PR into 6 parts, and PQ into 8 parts; the side length must be LCM(4,6,8)=24 for all parts to be integers.

given.aim: find the minimum area of equilateral triangle PQR

terms.meaning: the side length of QR must be divisible by 4
terms.example: if QR = 24, each part is 6 cm (integer)
terms.connects: contributes the factor 4 to the LCM constraint

terms.meaning: the side length of PR must be divisible by 6
terms.example: if PR = 24, each part is 4 cm (integer)
terms.connects: contributes the factor 6 to the LCM constraint

terms.meaning: the side length of PQ must be divisible by 8
terms.example: if PQ = 24, each part is 3 cm (integer)
terms.connects: contributes the factor 8 to the LCM constraint; since it is equilateral, all sides are equal

given.plan: Find the minimum side length a = LCM(4, 6, 8), then compute area = (sqrt(3)/4) * a^2.

steps.title: Find the minimum integer side length
steps.apply: 4 = 2^2, 6 = 2 * 3, 8 = 2^3. LCM takes the highest power of each prime: LCM = 2^3 * 3 = 24. So a = 24 cm.
steps.note: Checking: 24/4 = 6, 24/6 = 4, 24/8 = 3. All are integers, confirming a = 24 is valid.

steps.title: Compute the area of the equilateral triangle

questions.formula_note: The LCM computation uses prime-exponent rules (registry id: gcd-lcm-prime-exponents); the equilateral triangle area formula (sqrt(3)/4 * a^2) should be added to the registry as 'equilateral-triangle-area'.

formulas.name: Angle Bisector Length
formulas.plain: length of bisector from vertex A with adjacent sides b and c and half-angle A/2: t_a = (2bc / (b+c)) * cos(A/2)

formulas.name: Exterior Angle Theorem
formulas.plain: an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles

formulas.name: Area of an Equilateral Triangle
formulas.plain: Area = (sqrt(3) / 4) * a^2  where a is the side length
